walkthrough.title: Assemble the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get this party started! Grab the two board sections from the box. Place the smaller section — the one with the 2x, 3x, and 4x scoring zones — flat on the table. Now take the larger section with the 0x and 1x zones and line up the wooden dowels on its underside with the holes on the smaller section. Press them firmly together. You should now have a tiered ramp that steps down from a raised launch area at the back to the 4x zone at the very front. It should look like a little ski slope for dice!

walkthrough.title: Insert the Wooden Pegs
walkthrough.text: See those small holes along the edges of the board? Grab the 10 wooden dowels (pegs) and press one into each hole. These pegs act as bumpers and guardrails, keeping dice from flying off the sides too easily. Make sure each peg is snug — you don't want them popping out mid-flick!

walkthrough.title: Choose Colors and Distribute Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color. The game comes with 18 dice in four colors — two sets of 5 and two sets of 4. For 3 or 4 individual players, each player takes 4 dice of one color. For 2 players, each player takes 5 dice of one color. For team play with 4 players, form two teams of two and combine your colors. Grab a sheet of paper and a pencil for scorekeeping!

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Everyone rolls one die. Whoever rolls the highest number goes first, then the next highest, and so on. If there's a tie, those players roll again. The turn order matters — going later in a round means you can aim at opponents' dice that are already on the board! Write down the player order so nobody forgets.

walkthrough.title: Know the Board Zones
walkthrough.text: Take a quick look at the board from back to front. The raised platform at the very back is the launch zone — that's where you place your die before flicking. Then there's the 0x zone (before the dark scoring line), then the 1x, 2x, 3x, and 4x zones stepping down like a staircase. The further your die travels, the bigger the multiplier! A die showing 6 on the 4x zone scores a whopping 24 points — the maximum possible. A die that doesn't cross the dark line scores nothing. Got it? Let's roll!

summary.title: Assemble the Board
bullets: Place smaller section (2x/3x/4x) on a flat surface
Align wooden dowels from larger section (0x/1x) into the holes
Press firmly — board should form a tiered ramp

summary.title: Insert the Wooden Pegs
bullets: Press 10 wooden dowels into the holes along the board edges
These act as bumpers to keep dice on the board

summary.title: Choose Colors and Distribute Dice
bullets: Each player picks a color (18 dice total: two sets of 5, two sets of 4)
3-4 players: 4 dice each | 2 players: 5 dice each
Grab paper and pencil for scorekeeping

summary.title: Determine Turn Order
bullets: Everyone rolls one die — highest goes first
Ties re-roll to break the order
Write down the turn order

summary.title: Know the Board Zones
bullets: Launch zone (raised platform at back) — place die here before flicking
0x zone (before dark line) — scores nothing
1x, 2x, 3x, 4x zones — multiplier increases as you go further down
Max score per die: 6 x 4 = 24 points

walkthrough.title: On Your Turn: Flick a Die
walkthrough.text: When it's your turn, place one of your dice anywhere on the launch platform at the back of the board. Then flick, slide, or roll it forward with a finger-flick toward the scoring zones. The die MUST make contact with the launch platform first — no throwing it through the air! Your goal is to send it tumbling down the tiers and land it on the highest-multiplier zone you can. The face-up number on the die after it stops is what counts.

walkthrough.title: The Scoring Line
walkthrough.text: See that dark line printed across the middle of the board? That's the scoring line, and it's everything. Your die must COMPLETELY cross that line to score any points. If even a tiny bit of your die is touching the line or hasn't passed it, that die scores zero and is removed from the board. No mercy! Think of it as the velvet rope at a club — you're either in or you're out.

walkthrough.title: Bumping and Knocking
walkthrough.text: Here's where the chaos kicks in! When you flick your die, you can absolutely crash it into other dice — yours or your opponents'. Knock an opponent's die off the board? Beautiful. That die scores nothing for them. Push your own die into a better zone? Totally legal. You can even hit multiple dice in one shot. This is a dexterity game with attitude — bumping is not just allowed, it's encouraged!

walkthrough.title: Once a Die Stops, Hands Off
walkthrough.text: After you flick your die and everything settles, do NOT touch any dice on the board. Whether your die landed on the 4x zone or sailed right off the edge, it stays where it is (or goes to dice heaven if it fell off). Dice that remain on the board become obstacles and targets for future flicks. The board gets more crowded — and more strategic — with every turn.

walkthrough.title: Alternating Turns
walkthrough.text: Players take turns in the established order, each flicking one die per turn. You keep going around until every player has flicked all of their dice. Once everyone's dice have been launched, the round is over. Time to count up!

walkthrough.title: Scoring a Round
walkthrough.text: When the round ends, score every die still on the board that has fully crossed the scoring line. For each die, multiply the face-up number by the zone multiplier. A die showing 5 on the 3x zone? That's 15 points. A die showing 2 on the 1x zone? Just 2 points. A die showing 6 on the 4x zone? A glorious 24 — the maximum! Add up all your dice for your round total and write it down. Remove all dice from the board.

walkthrough.title: Next Round and Turn Order
walkthrough.text: After scoring, the player with the highest cumulative score goes first in the next round, followed by the next highest, and so on. If players are tied, their order reverts to the previous round's order. Reset the board — it should be completely clear of dice — and start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The standard game is 4 rounds. After all 4 rounds, add up every player's total score. Highest score wins! If there's a tie, play an overtime round with just the tied players to determine the champion. Alternatively, you can play to 301 points instead — keep playing rounds until someone hits 301. If multiple players pass 301 in the same round, the highest score wins. Pick whichever format your group prefers!

Die must contact the launch platform first — no airborne throws

Dice on or behind the line = 0 points, removed from board

Pushing your own die into a better zone is legal

Example: die showing 5 on 3x zone = 15 points
Max per die: 6 x 4 = 24 points
Sum all your dice for the round total

bullets: Highest cumulative scorer goes first next round

Clear all dice from the board before starting

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Standard: 4 rounds — highest total score wins
Tie after 4 rounds: play overtime round(s) with tied players
Alternative: play to 301 points instead of 4 rounds

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Ramp!
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's walk through a practice round of Tumblin' Dice! We've got three players at the table — Player 1 (Red), Player 2 (Blue), and Player 3 (Green). Each has 4 dice. The board is assembled, the pegs are in, and everyone's itching to start flicking. Let's see how this wild ride works!

walkthrough.title: Player 1's First Flick
walkthrough.text: Red places a die on the launch platform, takes aim at the 4x zone at the far end, and gives it a confident flick. The die tumbles down the tiers... and lands on the 2x zone showing a 4. That's 2 x 4 = 8 points so far. Not bad for a first shot! The die sits there on the board, waiting to see what happens next.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Opening Shot
walkthrough.text: Blue steps up, places a die on the launch platform, and flicks it forward. It rolls down smoothly and settles on the 3x zone showing a 5. That's 3 x 5 = 15 points — a great shot! Blue is off to a strong start.

walkthrough.title: Mistake #1: Too Much Power!
walkthrough.text: Green gets a little too excited. They wind up and LAUNCH their die with way too much force. The die rockets down the ramp, flies past every scoring zone, and sails right off the end of the board onto the table. Zero points. The die is out of play for this round. Ouch!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Control Your Flick
walkthrough.text: Big takeaway here — Tumblin' Dice is NOT about maximum power. A gentle, controlled flick beats a monster launch every time. The 4x zone is at the far end, yes, but the board isn't that long. Practice a smooth, medium-strength flick and you'll keep way more dice on the board. Dice that fly off score zero — that's the worst possible outcome!

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Flick: Going Aggressive
walkthrough.text: Red spots Blue's die sitting pretty on the 3x zone. Instead of just aiming for points, Red decides to play offense. Red places a die on the launch platform and aims directly at Blue's die. The flick connects — CRACK! Blue's die gets bumped off the 3x zone and tumbles off the board entirely. Red's die takes its place on the 3x zone, showing a 3. That's 3 x 3 = 9 points for Red, and Blue's 15-point die is gone! Savage but smart.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Recovers
walkthrough.text: Blue shakes it off and flicks the next die. This one settles on the 2x zone with a 6 showing. That's 2 x 6 = 12 points. Not as good as the 15 they just lost, but still solid. Blue is learning — the board state changes with every single flick.

walkthrough.title: Mistake #2: Ignoring the Competition
walkthrough.text: Green has learned to control their flick — great! But now Green just aims for empty space on the 2x zone, completely ignoring Red's die sitting on the 3x zone worth 9 points. Green's die lands on the 2x zone showing a 3, scoring 6 points. Meanwhile, Red's valuable die stays untouched. Green had a shot at knocking Red off and didn't even try!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play Defense Too!
walkthrough.text: Don't just focus on your own score — think about denying points to your opponents! If someone has a high-value die sitting on the 3x or 4x zone, seriously consider targeting it. Knocking a 6 off the 4x zone takes 24 points away from your opponent. That's often worth more than scoring 8 or 10 points in a safe spot. Offense wins rounds, but defense wins games!

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Third Flick: A Lucky Bounce
walkthrough.text: Red flicks another die down the ramp. It bumps off one of the wooden pegs on the side, changes direction, and rolls all the way to the 4x zone — showing a 6! That's 4 x 6 = 24 points! The maximum possible score on a single die! Red pumps a fist. Sometimes the bounces go your way, and that's part of the fun.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Goes for Revenge
walkthrough.text: Blue is NOT going to let that 24-pointer stand. Blue takes careful aim and flicks a die right at Red's 4x monster. The dice collide — Red's die gets bumped but only slides to the 3x zone. Blue's die ends up on the 4x zone showing a 2. Blue scored 8 points and reduced Red from 24 down to 18 (3 x 6). Not a perfect hit, but still a big swing!

walkthrough.title: Mistake #3: Only Aiming for 4x
walkthrough.text: Green has one die left and goes for glory — aiming exclusively at the 4x zone. The flick is decent, but the die barely clips the 4x zone, wobbles, and drops off the front edge of the board. Zero points again. Green was so fixated on the highest multiplier that they ignored perfectly good scoring opportunities on the 2x and 3x zones.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Take What the Board Gives You
walkthrough.text: The 4x zone is tempting, but it's a small target at the very edge of the board. A die showing 5 on the 3x zone is 15 points — that's still excellent! Consistently landing on the 2x and 3x zones will outscore someone who gets one lucky 4x hit but sends three dice off the board. Aim for high-multiplier zones, but be realistic about what your flick can reach safely.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: All dice have been flicked! Let's tally up. First, remove any dice that didn't fully cross the scoring line — they score zero. Now multiply each remaining die: face-up number times the zone it's in. Red has three dice on the board: 8 + 9 + 18 = 35 points (remember, the 24-pointer got bumped to 3x). Blue has two dice totaling 20 points. Green has just one die for 6 points. Red wins the round! Write down the scores and clear the board for round 2.

walkthrough.title: Next Round Order
walkthrough.text: Red scored highest, so Red goes first in round 2. Then Blue, then Green. Going first means your dice are open targets for longer, but you also get to set the tone. Going last means you can react to what's on the board. Both have advantages — that's what makes this game so fun round after round!

walkthrough.title: You're Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text:
You've seen the flicking, the bumping, the scoring, and the three biggest rookie mistakes — flicking too hard, ignoring defense, and tunnel-visioning the 4x zone. Remember: controlled flicks keep dice on the board, targeting opponents' dice is just as important as scoring your own, and the 2x and 3x zones are your bread and butter.

Check out General Tips for more strategy, and peek at the Appendix for a quick reference on scoring and variants.

Most importantly — have a blast! Tumblin' Dice is meant to be loud, fun, and full of epic moments. Ask your GM Guide any questions during play. Now get flicking!

Check General Tips and the Appendix for more

walkthrough.title: Master Your Flick Consistency
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Tumblin' Dice is a consistent, repeatable flick. Don't worry about power — worry about control. Practice flicking the same way every time so you can predict where your die will end up. Use the pad of your finger, not the nail, and follow through smoothly. Players who can reliably land dice on the 2x and 3x zones will crush players who occasionally spike a 4x but send half their dice off the board.

walkthrough.title: High Die Faces Matter More Than You Think
walkthrough.text: The die face is half the equation. A 6 on the 2x zone scores 12 points — the same as a 4 on the 3x zone. You can't control what number lands face-up, but you can appreciate that every multiplier zone becomes more valuable with higher die faces. Don't get discouraged by a die landing on 1x — if it shows a 6, that's still 6 respectable points!

walkthrough.title: Target High-Value Opponents First
walkthrough.text: When deciding whether to aim for your own score or attack an opponent, do the math. If someone has a 6 on the 4x zone (24 points), knocking that off the board is worth way more than landing a 3 on the 2x zone yourself (6 points). Always scan the board before flicking and identify the biggest threats. Removing a 20+ point die is often the highest-value play you can make.

walkthrough.title: Use the Board Geometry
walkthrough.text: The tiered board creates natural stopping points where the levels change. Dice tend to settle right at the edges of tiers. Use this to your advantage — aim for the transition between zones, and your die is likely to stop on the higher-multiplier side. The wooden pegs along the edges can also redirect your die, so factor in bank shots when planning your flick.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Best Dice
walkthrough.text: Got a 5 or 6 sitting on the 3x or 4x zone? Use your next die strategically — place it in front of or beside your valuable die to act as a shield. Opponents who try to knock off your high scorer might hit your blocker instead. It doesn't always work, but a well-placed bodyguard die can protect huge points.

walkthrough.title: Don't Forget About the 1x Zone
walkthrough.text: The 1x zone doesn't look exciting, but a die that scores 4, 5, or 6 points is infinitely better than one that's off the board scoring zero. If the 2x, 3x, and 4x zones are crowded with opponent dice and you don't have a clean shot, a safe landing in the 1x zone still puts points on the board. Don't overthink it — some points are better than no points.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Turn Order Awareness
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to who flicks after you. If you go early in the round, your dice are exposed to more attacks. Consider less aggressive placements when you go first — a die on the 2x zone is less tempting to target than one on the 4x. When you go last, nobody can bump your die for the rest of the round, so that's the time to go for glory on the 4x zone!

summary.title: Master Your Flick Consistency
bullets: Control > power — practice a smooth, repeatable flick
Use the finger pad, not the nail, and follow through
Consistent 2x/3x landings beat occasional 4x spikes

summary.title: High Die Faces Matter More Than You Think
bullets: A 6 on 2x (12 pts) = a 4 on 3x (12 pts)
Higher die faces make every zone more valuable
Don't despair over 1x landings — a 6 on 1x is still 6 points

summary.title: Target High-Value Opponents First
bullets: Scan the board before every flick for big targets
Knocking off a 24-point die > scoring 6 points yourself
Do the math: attack value vs. your likely scoring value

summary.title: Use the Board Geometry
bullets: Dice naturally settle at tier transitions — aim for edges
Wooden pegs can redirect dice — plan bank shots
Gravity and friction work differently at each tier

summary.title: Protect Your Best Dice
bullets: Place a die in front of your high-value die as a shield
Blocker dice can absorb attacks meant for your scorer
Especially useful when you have a 5 or 6 on the 3x/4x zone

summary.title: Don't Forget About the 1x Zone
bullets: A die scoring 5 on 1x > a die off the board scoring 0
Safe 1x landings add up over multiple rounds
Use 1x when higher zones are too crowded or risky

summary.title: Manage Your Turn Order Awareness
bullets: Going early? Play safer — your dice are exposed longer
Going last? Go for 4x — nobody can bump you afterward
Adjust aggression based on how many players follow you

walkthrough.title: The Two-for-One Shot
walkthrough.text: Advanced players look for two-for-one opportunities on every flick. Can you knock an opponent's die off the board AND land yours on a scoring zone in the same shot? This requires reading angles and understanding how dice transfer momentum. Aim to hit the side of an opponent's die so yours deflects into a scoring position while theirs goes flying. When it works, it's the most satisfying play in the game.

walkthrough.title: Crowding the 4x Zone
walkthrough.text: If you land a die on the 4x zone early, your next die should land right next to it — even if it only scores modestly. Why? A cluster of your dice on the 4x zone is harder to clear in one shot. Your opponent has to use multiple dice to knock them all off, which means fewer dice aimed at scoring their own points. Turn the 4x zone into your fortress.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponent Tendencies
walkthrough.text: After a round or two, you'll notice patterns. Some players always aim for the same zone. Some always try to bump. Some are timid and play safe. Exploit this information! If your opponent always targets the 4x zone, place a die there as bait while your real scorer sits quietly on the 3x. If they never play defense, stack up points without fear of attack.

walkthrough.title: The Sacrifice Play
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the smartest move is intentionally sending your die into a kamikaze run. If an opponent has 40+ points worth of dice on the board and you have a low-value die left, don't bother scoring — aim to cause maximum chaos. Even if your die ends up off the board, clearing 20 or 30 points of opponents' dice is a massive net win for you in relative terms.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Flick Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Remember that the highest scorer goes first next round. Sometimes you don't WANT to go first — going last is strategically powerful because nobody can retaliate against your dice. In the later rounds, consider whether it's worth sandbagging a little to get a more favorable turn order. Going last with four dice when the score is close can be game-winning.

walkthrough.title: Bank Shots Off the Pegs
walkthrough.text: The wooden pegs aren't just guardrails — they're tools. An advanced player can intentionally angle their flick to bounce off a peg and redirect toward a specific target or zone. This is especially useful when there's a direct-line obstacle between you and your target. Practice peg shots in warm-ups and you'll have a secret weapon nobody expects.

walkthrough.title: Psychological Warfare
walkthrough.text: This is a party game — table talk is part of the fun! Announce that you're targeting someone's big die to make them nervous. Or bluff that you're going for a bump when you're really just scoring. When opponents are mentally flustered, their flicks suffer. Celebrate big shots loudly to build momentum and keep the pressure on. In a dexterity game, confidence is a real advantage.

summary.title: The Two-for-One Shot
bullets: Knock an opponent's die off while landing yours on a scoring zone
Hit the side of their die to deflect yours into position
Highest-value single play in the game when executed

summary.title: Crowding the 4x Zone
bullets: Cluster your dice on 4x to make them harder to clear
Opponents must spend multiple dice to break up your group
Even a low die face on 4x forces a response

summary.title: Reading Opponent Tendencies
bullets: Track where each player aims and how they play
Exploit predictable patterns with baiting and counter-targeting
Timid opponents = safe high-zone plays for you

summary.title: The Sacrifice Play
bullets: Use a low-value die as a wrecking ball into crowded zones
Clearing 20+ opponent points is worth losing your die
Best used when opponent has a cluster of high-value dice

summary.title: Endgame Flick Order Manipulation
bullets: Going last = no retaliation against your dice
Consider sandbagging slightly to avoid going first
Especially powerful in the final round with a close score

summary.title: Bank Shots Off the Pegs
bullets: Use wooden pegs as intentional redirect angles
Great for reaching targets behind obstacles
Practice in warm-ups to develop this skill

summary.title: Psychological Warfare
bullets: Announce targets to create pressure and anxiety
Bluff offensive plays to throw opponents off their game
Celebrate big shots — confidence is contagious in dexterity games

entries.definition: The raised area at the back of the board where you place your die before flicking. Your die must start here and make contact with this surface first. Also called the landing disc or launch zone.
The dark horizontal line printed across the middle of the board. A die must completely cross this line to score any points. Dice touching or behind the line score zero and are removed.
The area of the board between the launch platform and the scoring line. Dice that stop here score zero points and are removed at the end of the round.
The first scoring zone past the scoring line. The die's face-up value is multiplied by 1 (so a 5 scores 5 points). The safest landing area but the lowest multiplier.
The second scoring tier. The die's face-up value is multiplied by 2 (so a 5 scores 10 points). A reliable target for consistent scoring.
The third scoring tier. The die's face-up value is multiplied by 3 (so a 5 scores 15 points). High reward with moderate risk.
The highest scoring tier at the very front of the board. The die's face-up value is multiplied by 4 (so a 6 scores 24 — the maximum). High reward but risky since dice can easily fly off the edge.
Small wooden cylinders inserted into holes along the board edges. They act as bumpers and guardrails. Advanced players use them for intentional bank shots.

The primary action in the game — using a finger to propel your die from the launch platform toward the scoring zones. Can be a slide, roll, or snap of the finger. The die must contact the launch platform first.
Hitting another die (yours or an opponent's) with your flicked die. Knocking opponents' dice off the board denies them points. Bumping your own die into a better zone is also legal.
A die that has fallen or been knocked off the playing surface. It scores zero points and is out of play for the remainder of the round.
One complete cycle where every player has flicked all of their dice. The standard game consists of 4 rounds. Scores are tallied at the end of each round.
The sequence in which players flick. Initially determined by a die roll. In subsequent rounds, the player with the highest cumulative score goes first. Ties revert to previous round order.
The number showing on the top face of a die after it comes to rest. This is the number that gets multiplied by the zone value for scoring. Standard dice show 1 through 6.
The highest possible score from a single die is 24 points: a 6 on the face multiplied by the 4x zone. This is the dream shot!
An extra round played when two or more players are tied after the standard 4 rounds. Only the tied players participate. Additional overtime rounds are played until the tie is broken.

An alternative game mode where instead of playing 4 rounds, players keep playing rounds until someone reaches or exceeds 301 points. If multiple players pass 301 in the same round, the highest score wins.
A variant for 4 or 6 players. Teams of two or three share colors and add their scores together. Teammates alternate turns with opponents — they never shoot back to back.
For younger players who haven't learned multiplication, add the die value to the zone value instead of multiplying. Example: a 4 on the 3x zone scores 4 + 3 = 7 instead of 12.
An advanced variant where dice knocked off the board count as negative points for their owner. This makes defensive play even more rewarding and adds tension to every flick.
In a 2-player game, each player takes 5 dice of one color plus 1 bonus die of another color. Points scored by the bonus die count double. You choose when to flick your bonus die.
An optional variant where a player earns bonus points (+10, +20, or double) if all their dice remain on the board at the end of a round. The specific bonus is agreed upon before the game.
An advanced technique where you intentionally flick your die into a wooden peg to redirect it toward a target or scoring zone. Requires practice but can bypass obstacles.
A die placed strategically to shield a higher-value die from being bumped by opponents. Even a low-scoring die can serve as a valuable bodyguard.
Intentionally sending your die into a cluster of opponent dice with no concern for your own score. The goal is to cause maximum disruption and deny points to opponents.
